html:
[843]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 浣纱西子 - Refers to Xi Shi, the legendary beauty of the Spring and Autumn period, who was a concubine of King Goujian of Yue. The phrase '浣纱' (washing silk) is associated with her.
  • 千百年后竟不一见 - Means 'not seen even once after thousands of years,' expressing rarity or uniqueness.
  • 两间恨事也 - Means 'a regrettable matter in the world,' indicating a significant disappointment or lament.
  • 不意柳于仓卒中遇之 - Means 'unexpectedly, Liu met her in a hurry,' emphasizing the serendipity of the encounter.
  • 且其人之美艳，既不少逊于夷光 - Means 'and her beauty was no less than that of Xi Shi,' comparing her beauty to Xi Shi's.
  • 一室倡随，百年偕老 - Means 'living together in harmony, growing old together for a hundred years,' describing a long and happy marriage.
  • 胜于吴之为沼蠡之泛湖多多矣 - Means 'far surpassing the fate of Wu becoming a swamp and Xi Shi floating on a lake,' referring to the tragic end of King Goujian and Xi Shi.
  • 独狐翁以术饵其婿 - Means 'Old Man Du used deceit to lure his son-in-law,' indicating a manipulative act.
  • 柳生以智蔽其亲 - Means 'Liu used intelligence to deceive his parents,' suggesting a clever but deceptive action.
  • 冰清玉润者，曾如是乎？ - Means 'Is it like this for one who is pure and smooth like ice and jade?' questioning the moral integrity of the characters.
  • 微姑之高义，好合虽可成，人言良可畏也 - Means 'Without the high virtue of the aunt, even a good match could not be achieved, for people's words are indeed to be feared,' highlighting the importance of moral support.
  • 谋事在人，成事在天 - Means 'Planning is up to man, but success is up to heaven,' expressing the idea that while effort is important, ultimate success depends on fate.
  • 两美之合，又无非苍天之意也夫 - Means 'The union of two beauties is also nothing but the will of heaven,' suggesting that their union is divinely ordained.
  • 随园老人曰 - Means 'The old man of the Su Garden said,' introducing the speaker.
  • 武夷九曲，使人历尽方知 - Means 'The nine bends of Wuyi Mountain can only be fully understood after experiencing them,' referring to the scenic beauty of Wuyi Mountain.
  • 初见之，但有奇峰壁立耳 - Means 'At first glance, there are only towering peaks,' describing the initial impression of the mountain.
  • 似无可转之境也 - Means 'It seems like there is no turning back,' suggesting a sense of finality or inevitability.
  • 何物文心，竟与山灵争胜 - Means 'What kind of literary heart can compete with the spirit of the mountain?' questioning the ability of human creativity to match natural beauty.
  • 吾于此，又得其不可解之一 - Means 'In this, I find another thing that is inexplicable,' expressing a sense of mystery or wonder.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浣纱西子 - Xi Shi, the legendary beauty of the Spring and Autumn period, known for her beauty and loyalty.
  • 吴之为沼蠡之泛湖 - Refers to the tragic fate of King Goujian of Yue, who, after his defeat, had Xi Shi drown herself in a lake.
  • 独狐翁 - An old man named Du, likely a character in the story.
  • 柳生 - Liu, a character in the story, possibly a suitor or a relative.
  • 冰清玉润 - A metaphor for moral purity and integrity, often used to describe virtuous individuals.
  • 随园老人 - The old man of the Su Garden, a figure of wisdom and experience.
  • 武夷九曲 - The nine bends of Wuyi Mountain, a famous scenic area in Fujian Province, known for its natural beauty and cultural significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 浣纱西子 - Xi Shi, the legendary beauty.
  • 吴之为沼蠡之泛湖 - The tragic fate of King Goujian and Xi Shi.
  • 独狐翁 - Old Man Du.
  • 柳生 - Liu.
  • 随园老人 - The old man of the Su Garden.
  • 武夷九曲 - The nine bends of Wuyi Mountain.

[844]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 酕眗 - Classical term meaning 'intoxicated' or 'drunk,' often used to describe a state of deep inebriation.
  • 仗一白梃 - Literally 'wielding a white stick,' referring to a wooden staff or club, often used as a weapon or tool.
  • 重乔之矛 - A metaphorical description of a spear, possibly alluding to a specific type of spear or its appearance.
  • 夜巡者 - Refers to night patrol officers or guards.
  • 捕盗之具 - Means 'tools for catching thieves' or 'equipment for apprehending criminals.'
  • 谛观之 - Means 'to look closely' or 'to observe carefully.'
  • 某人之室 - Refers to 'a certain person's house' or 'a specific house.'
  • 新婚未久 - Means 'recently married' or 'not married for long.'
  • 心偶动 - Means 'the heart suddenly moves' or 'the mind is suddenly aroused.'
  • 属耳于垣 - Means 'to listen closely to the wall' or 'to eavesdrop on the wall.'
  • 狎昵之状 - Refers to 'an intimate and affectionate state' or 'a close and affectionate relationship.'
  • 筹思 - Means 'to plan' or 'to deliberate.'
  • 挹怏而前 - Means 'to advance with a sense of dissatisfaction' or 'to move forward with a feeling of discontent.'
  • 蠕蠕然行 - Describes 'crawling or moving slowly.'
  • 来甚蹇缓 - Means 'coming very slowly' or 'moving very slowly.'
  • 衣装约略可辨 - Means 'clothing can be vaguely recognized' or 'clothing can be roughly identified.'
  • 良家 - Refers to 'a respectable family' or 'a decent household.'
  • 似犹可挑 - Means 'it seems still possible to seduce' or 'it seems still possible to approach.'
  • 尾之 - Means 'to follow' or 'to trail.'
  • 径入一巷 - Means 'to enter a lane directly' or 'to go straight into a lane.'
  • 荜门甚低矮 - Describes 'a low and humble gate' or 'a low and modest gate.'
  • 侧身入内 - Means 'to enter sideways' or 'to enter by sidling in.'
  • 屏息伺之 - Means 'to hold one's breath and wait' or 'to wait silently and attentively.'
  • 群儿嬉戏声 - Refers to 'the sound of children playing' or 'the noise of children playing.'
  • 依依孺慕 - Means 'affectionate and fond' or 'loving and attached.'
  • 无壮夫 - Means 'no strong men' or 'no adult males.'
  • 心乃安 - Means 'the heart then calms' or 'the mind then becomes at ease.'
  • 窥有短垣 - Means 'to spy on a short wall' or 'to look for a short wall.'
  • 因逾焉 - Means 'therefore, he climbed over it' or 'he then climbed over it.'
  • 叱问伊谁 - Means 'to shout and ask who it is' or 'to call out and inquire who it is.'
  • 依钩于壁 - Means 'to lean the hook against the wall' or 'to place the hook against the wall.'
  • 突入其室 - Means 'to suddenly enter the room' or 'to rush into the room.'
  • 高二也 - Means 'It's Gao Er' or 'It's Gao Er.'
  • 以钩来乎 - Means 'Did you come with the hook?' or 'Did you bring the hook with you?'
  • 植于墙下 - Means 'planted at the foot of the wall' or 'placed at the base of the wall.'
  • 巧笑承迎 - Means 'to smile cleverly and welcome' or 'to greet with a clever smile.'
  • 若不峻拒 - Means 'as if not harshly refusing' or 'as if not strongly rejecting.'
  • 稚子辈未免扰攘 - Means 'the children are inevitably noisy' or 'the children are unavoidably noisy.'
  • 请君与妾升此屋 - Means 'please come up to this house with me' or 'please ascend this house with me.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely referring to the capital of the Eastern Han dynasty, Luoyang.
  • 逻卒 - Refers to night patrol officers or guards.
  • 高二 - The name of the main character, a night patrol officer.
  • 某人之室 - Refers to a specific house, though the identity of the owner is not specified.
  • 新婚未久 - Indicates a recent marriage, which may have been a common event in the social context of the time.
  • 夜巡 - Refers to night patrols, a common practice in ancient Chinese cities for maintaining order and security.
  • 捕盗之具 - Refers to the tools used by night patrol officers to catch thieves or criminals.

Proper Nouns

  • 京师 - The capital city.
  • 逻卒 - Night patrol officers.
  • 高二 - The main character, a night patrol officer.
  • 某人之室 - A specific house, though the identity of the owner is not specified.

[845]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 若抱瓮，若拥肿 - Metaphorical descriptions of the woman's body, likening it to holding a jar (瓮) or a swollen object (拥肿), suggesting a round and full shape.
  • 颇不可耐 - Means 'quite unbearable' or 'very uncomfortable,' but in this context, it likely implies a sense of overwhelming pleasure.
  • 为欢良久 - Means 'they enjoyed themselves for a long time.'
  • 甚惬素心 - Means 'very satisfying to their original intentions' or 'very fulfilling their desires.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 高二哥 - Refers to the man named 'Gao Er Ge,' a character in the story.
  • 小豝 - Refers to a young pig, suggesting that the man might have been trying to steal a pig.
  • 圈上 - Refers to a pigsty or pigpen, indicating the setting where the man woke up.
  • 豢豕之牢 - Refers to a place where pigs are kept and raised, reinforcing the setting of a pigpen.

Proper Nouns

  • 高二哥 - The name of the man in the story.
  • 小豝 - A young pig, a specific item mentioned in the story.
  • 圈上 - The location where the man wakes up, a pigpen.
  • 豢豕之牢 - The place where pigs are kept and raised, reinforcing the setting of a pigpen.

[846]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俯而窥之 - Literal phrase meaning 'stooped to look at it,' describing the act of bending down to observe something.
  • 齁然假寐 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'snoring while pretending to sleep,' indicating a state of semi-consciousness or drowsiness.
  • 己之钩，实在焉 - Literal phrase meaning 'one's own hook was indeed there,' indicating the presence of a specific object.
  • 不觉大惊 - Literal phrase meaning 'unconsciously greatly startled,' describing a sudden and involuntary reaction.
  • 身无寸缕 - Literal phrase meaning 'no thread of clothing on one's body,' indicating a state of being completely naked.
  • 羞涩不胜 - Literal phrase meaning 'shame and embarrassment beyond endurance,' describing an intense feeling of shame and discomfort.
  • 赧然而下 - Literal phrase meaning 'ashamed and embarrassed to descend,' indicating a reluctant or embarrassed action.
  • 初主人见其为二 - Literal phrase meaning 'at first, the owner saw it as two,' describing an initial perception of something as being in pairs or multiples.
  • 且形状如狂 - Literal phrase meaning 'and the shape was like madness,' indicating an appearance that seems chaotic or irrational.
  • 亦愕然 - Literal phrase meaning 'also greatly startled,' describing a reaction of surprise or shock.
  • 呼而与语 - Literal phrase meaning 'called out and spoke with,' indicating a direct interaction or conversation.
  • 其侪二人亦先在 - Literal phrase meaning 'the two companions were also already there,' describing the presence of others.
  • 争诘之 - Literal phrase meaning 'argued and questioned him,' indicating a confrontational or interrogative interaction.
  • 二实内怍 - Literal phrase meaning 'the two were indeed internally ashamed,' describing a feeling of internal guilt or shame.
  • 不能言 - Literal phrase meaning 'unable to speak,' indicating a state of being speechless or unable to articulate.
  • 是豕老矣 - Literal phrase meaning 'this pig is old,' describing the age of a pig.
  • 不烹之，将为妖 - Literal phrase meaning 'if not cooked, it will become a demon,' indicating a superstitious belief that an uncooked animal can cause harm.
  • 众始悟其与豕为好 - Literal phrase meaning 'the crowd finally realized that it was friendly with the pig,' indicating a realization of a relationship or association.
  • 不禁大笑 - Literal phrase meaning 'could not help but laugh,' describing an uncontrollable reaction of laughter.
  • 二竟不顾其钩 - Literal phrase meaning 'the two finally paid no attention to their hook,' indicating a disregard for a specific object.
  • 匆遽而遁 - Literal phrase meaning 'hurriedly and hastily fled,' describing a quick and urgent departure.
  • 翌日 - Literal phrase meaning 'the next day,' indicating a temporal reference to the following day.
  • 主人果烹此豕 - Literal phrase meaning 'the owner indeed cooked this pig,' describing an action taken by the owner.
  • 二梦前妇欣欣而来 - Literal phrase meaning 'the two dreamed of the previous woman coming happily,' indicating a dream involving a past female figure.
  • 赖君片言解脱 - Literal phrase meaning 'relying on your few words to解脱 (release or free),' describing a situation where someone is freed from a predicament through the words of another.
  • 今得转轮为人矣 - Literal phrase meaning 'now has been reborn as a person,' indicating a transformation or rebirth into a human form.
  • 惊觉 - Literal phrase meaning 'startled awake,' describing a sudden awakening from a dream.
  • 始举以告人 - Literal phrase meaning 'began to recount to others,' indicating the act of sharing information with others.
  • 闻之者，莫不捧腹也 - Literal phrase meaning 'those who heard it, none did not laugh heartily,' describing a widespread reaction of laughter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The act of stooping to look at something, which may be a common gesture in Chinese culture.
  • The description of snoring while pretending to sleep, which may be a common behavior in Chinese culture.
  • The mention of a hook, which may be a common tool in Chinese culture.
  • The description of being completely naked, which may be a common state in Chinese culture.
  • The description of shame and embarrassment, which may be a common emotion in Chinese culture.
  • The description of being ashamed and embarrassed to descend, which may be a common reaction in Chinese culture.
  • The initial perception of something as being in pairs or multiples, which may be a common observation in Chinese culture.
  • The description of an appearance that seems chaotic or irrational, which may be a common observation in Chinese culture.
  • The reaction of surprise or shock, which may be a common reaction in Chinese culture.
  • The act of calling out and speaking with someone, which may be a common interaction in Chinese culture.
  • The presence of companions, which may be a common social dynamic in Chinese culture.
  • The act of arguing and questioning, which may be a common interaction in Chinese culture.
  • The feeling of internal guilt or shame, which may be a common emotion in Chinese culture.
  • The state of being speechless or unable to articulate, which may be a common state in Chinese culture.
  • The description of an old pig, which may be a common animal in Chinese culture.
  • The superstitious belief that an uncooked animal can cause harm, which may be a common belief in Chinese culture.
  • The realization of a relationship or association, which may be a common observation in Chinese culture.
  • The uncontrollable reaction of laughter, which may be a common reaction in Chinese culture.
  • The disregard for a specific object, which may be a common behavior in Chinese culture.
  • The quick and urgent departure, which may be a common action in Chinese culture.
  • The temporal reference to the following day, which may be a common way of indicating time in Chinese culture.
  • The action of cooking a pig, which may be a common activity in Chinese culture.
  • The dream involving a past female figure, which may be a common dream in Chinese culture.
  • The situation where someone is freed from a predicament through the words of another, which may be a common belief in Chinese culture.
  • The transformation or rebirth into a human form, which may be a common belief in Chinese culture.
  • The sudden awakening from a dream, which may be a common experience in Chinese culture.
  • The act of sharing information with others, which may be a common behavior in Chinese culture.
  • The widespread reaction of laughter, which may be a common reaction in Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a scene where someone stoops to look at a large pig (彘母) that is snoring while pretending to sleep in the corner of a wall. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the vivid imagery.
  • This sentence describes the discovery of one's own hook and the sudden and involuntary reaction of being greatly startled. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the intensity of the reaction.
  • This sentence describes the state of being completely naked and the intense feeling of shame and discomfort. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the emotional impact.
  • This sentence describes the act of being ashamed and embarrassed to descend. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the emotional nuance.
  • This sentence describes the initial perception of something as being in pairs or multiples and the reaction of surprise or shock. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the vivid imagery.
  • This sentence describes the act of calling out and speaking with someone, the presence of companions, and the act of arguing and questioning. The sentence is clear but may require careful translation to maintain the dynamic interaction.
  • This sentence describes the feeling of internal guilt or shame and the state of being speechless or unable to articulate.

[847]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - Conventional formula used to introduce commentary or historical reflection, often attributed to an anonymous historian or chronicler.
  • 孟野之歌 - Refers to a song or poem attributed to a person named Meng Ye, likely a fictional or historical figure whose work is cited here.
  • 既定尔娄猪，盍归我艾豭 - Couplet from the song, where '娄猪' (lo pig) and '艾豭' (ai boar) are metaphors for a woman and a man, respectively. The phrase suggests a playful or satirical tone, possibly referring to a romantic or social situation.
  • 盍归我艾豭 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why not return to me, my boar?' The use of '艾豭' as a metaphor for a man adds a layer of humor or irony.
  • 二既以身为艾暇 - Phrase that is somewhat ambiguous, but it likely means 'Both have already taken themselves as boars,' suggesting that both parties have assumed a certain role or identity.
  • 娄猪不难定矣 - Phrase that means 'It is not difficult to settle the lo pig,' implying that the situation involving the woman (娄猪) can be resolved or managed.
  • 第恨主人当时误从一言 - Phrase that means 'But I regret that the host at the time was misled by one word,' indicating a sense of regret or disappointment due to a mistake.
  • 径投之鼎镬 - Phrase that means 'was immediately thrown into the cauldron,' a metaphor for being subjected to severe punishment or execution.
  • 不然，子朝方来，南子未老 - Conditional phrase that means 'Otherwise, if Zi Chao had just arrived, and Nanzi was not yet old,' suggesting a hypothetical scenario where things might have turned out differently.
  • 其所生又必有异矣 - Phrase that means 'what she bore would certainly be different,' implying that the outcome of the situation would have been different if the initial mistake had not occurred.
  • 奇遇 - Means 'a strange encounter' or 'a peculiar meeting,' setting the tone for the anecdote that follows.
  • 余又闻之某公 - Formulaic phrase meaning 'I heard from a certain official,' introducing the anecdote as a second-hand account.
  • 西陲荡定后 - Means 'after the western border was pacified,' referring to a period of stability following a military campaign.
  • 有军将秩且四品 - Means 'there was a military general of the fourth rank,' indicating the rank and status of the military leader.
  • 部其众往戍回疆 - Means 'he led his troops to garrison the Hui region,' describing the military action.
  • 临溪配渡 - Means 'they were crossing a river near a stream,' describing the setting of the event.
  • 独乘一舟 - Means 'riding alone in a boat,' indicating the solitary nature of the military leader's journey.
  • 回人年且耄 - Means 'an old Hui person,' describing the age and ethnicity of the person operating the boat.
  • 为之操楫 - Means 'operating the paddle,' indicating the role of the old Hui person.
  • 偶闻军将乡音 - Means 'he happened to hear the general's native accent,' suggesting a connection or familiarity.
  • 忽操汉语咨询曰 - Means 'suddenly spoke Chinese and asked,' indicating the old Hui person's ability to speak Chinese and his curiosity.
  • 公等俱自华土来 - Means 'all of you are from the Central Plains,' referring to the general's homeland.
  • 风景近复何似 - Means 'how is the scenery like these days?' a polite and culturally appropriate way to inquire about the homeland.
  • 阖舟闻而骇 - Means 'everyone on the boat was startled,' indicating the surprise and shock of the others.
  • 竞诘之 - Means 'they all questioned him,' showing the curiosity and interest of the others.
  • 乃泫然曰 - Means 'he then wept and said,' indicating the emotional state of the old Hui person.
  • 予虽居于此 - Means 'I have lived here,' indicating his current residence.
  • 种类实异 - Means 'but my lineage is different,' suggesting a different background or origin.
  • 身本中州世族也 - Means 'I am originally from a noble family in the Central Plains,' indicating his true heritage.
  • 少年入伍，随征殊方 - Means 'I joined the army when I was young and followed the campaign to distant lands,' describing his military service.
  • 一时偶失利 - Means 'at one time, I happened to suffer a defeat,' indicating a specific event in his past.
  • 遂陷于准噶尔部中 - Means 'and was captured by the Dzungar tribe,' describing his capture.
  • 渠役之如奴隶 - Means 'they treated me like a slave,' indicating the harsh conditions he endured.
  • 啮雪吞毡 - Means 'eating snow and swallowing felt,' a metaphor for enduring extreme hardship.
  • 苟延残喘 - Means 'barely surviving,' indicating the precarious state of his life.
  • 渠又售我于回部 - Means 'they then sold me to the Hui region,' describing his subsequent sale.
  • 遂习其俗，不能复变 - Means 'I then adopted their customs and could not change back,' indicating the lasting impact of his experiences.
  • 今又数十年 - Means 'it has been many years,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 窃幸皇威远震 - Means 'I secretly hope that the emperor's power is far-reaching,' expressing a wish for the emperor's influence.
  • 复睹大邦人物 - Means 'to see again the people of the great country,' indicating a sense of nostalgia and longing.
  • 不禁感而失言 - Means 'I cannot help but feel and speak out,' indicating the emotional release of the old Hui person.
  • 万勿见过 - Means 'please do not see it as a fault,' a plea for understanding and forgiveness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外史氏曰 - Common formula in Chinese historical texts, often used to introduce commentary or reflection.
  • 孟野之歌 - Reference to a song or poem attributed to a person named Meng Ye, which may be a fictional or historical figure.
  • 娄猪 - Metaphor for a woman, often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to a woman in a romantic or social context.
  • 艾豭 - Metaphor for a man, often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to a man in a romantic or social context.
  • 准噶尔部 - Refers to the Dzungar tribe, a powerful nomadic group in Central Asia during the Qing dynasty.
  • 回部 - Refers to the Hui region, a part of Xinjiang that was under Hui control during the Qing dynasty.
  • 中州 - Refers to the Central Plains, the heartland of Chinese civilization.
  • 世族 - Refers to a noble family, indicating a high social status.
  • 皇威远震 - Refers to the emperor's power being far-reaching, indicating the central authority of the Qing dynasty.

[848]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽动念 - Suddenly had a thought or suddenly came to mind.
  • 复以里族叩之 - Again asked about his family and clan.
  • 姓氏乡邦实与军将若合符节 - His surname, hometown, and place of origin matched the soldier’s as if they were made to fit together perfectly.
  • 瞿然甚惊 - Startled greatly.
  • 授室 - A euphemism for 'married.'
  • 琴瑟甚调 - A metaphor for a harmonious marriage, literally 'the zither and se played in harmony.'
  • 抱子 - To have a child.
  • 周岁 - One year old.
  • 枣梨未觅 - A metaphor for not yet having a child, literally 'not yet finding dates and plums.'
  • 噭然大恸 - Cried out in great sorrow.
  • 膝行而前 - Moved forward on his knees.
  • 愕然 - Startled or astonished.
  • 释棹而跪 - Let go of the paddle and knelt.
  • 凿凿言之 - Spoke firmly and clearly.
  • 质以祖祢名讳 - Asked for the names of his ancestors.
  • 无不吻合 - All matched perfectly.
  • 奉之同至戍所 - Took him along to the garrison.
  • 具牒于上官 - Prepared a report for the higher authorities.
  • 缕陈其实 - Detailed the facts.
  • 兼缴官诰赎父罪 - Also submitted official documents to redeem his father’s crime.
  • 宥而弗问 - Pardoned without further inquiry.
  • 兼听还其邻里 - Also allowed him to return to his community.
  • 太夫人犹在堂 - His mother was still alive.
  • 夫妇握手涕零 - The husband and wife held hands and wept.
  • 皆年逾七十 - Both were over seventy years old.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 军将 - Refers to a military officer or soldier.
  • 里族 - Refers to one's family and clan.
  • 姓氏乡邦 - Refers to one's surname, hometown, and place of origin.
  • 琴瑟甚调 - A metaphor for a harmonious marriage, derived from the traditional Chinese musical instruments zither and se.
  • 周岁 - Refers to a child being one year old.
  • 枣梨未觅 - A metaphor for not yet having a child, using the imagery of not finding dates and plums.
  • 噭然大恸 - Describes a loud and heartfelt cry of sorrow.
  • 膝行而前 - Describes moving forward on one's knees, often used in contexts of deep respect or submission.
  • 释棹而跪 - Describes letting go of the paddle and kneeling, indicating a change from a casual to a formal or respectful posture.
  • 凿凿言之 - Describes speaking firmly and clearly, often used to indicate certainty or conviction.
  • 祖祢名讳 - Refers to the names of one's ancestors, particularly the paternal grandfather and father.
  • 官诰 - Refers to official documents or decrees.
  • 宥而弗问 - Describes being pardoned without further inquiry, indicating a lenient or forgiving attitude from the authorities.
  • 兼听还其邻里 - Describes being allowed to return to one's community, indicating a restoration of social status or freedom.
  • 太夫人 - Refers to a woman of high social status, often used to address a mother or a respected older woman.
  • 涕零 - Means 'tears fell,' indicating weeping or crying.
  • 年逾七十 - Refers to being over seventy years old, indicating advanced age.

[849]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遇之奇者 - Literal: 'the strange encounter,' referring to unusual or unexpected events.
  • 倫紀之地 - Refers to the realm of family and social relationships, emphasizing moral and familial duties.

  • 及鬼事泄，历供前案 - When the ghost's involvement was revealed, he recounted all previous incidents, indicating a full disclosure.
  • 凡攫取数家 - He had taken several families, showing the extent of the ghost's actions.
  • 皆惊之使避 - He frightened them away, describing the ghost's method of operation.
  • 以快其胠箧之私 - To satisfy his own hidden desires, revealing the ghost's motivations.
  • 惟翁家畏鬼殊甚 - Only the old man's family feared the ghost especially, highlighting the family's unique situation.
  • 且室多丽质 - And their home had many beautiful women, explaining why the ghost targeted them.
  • 故不免于包羞 - Thus, they could not escape the shame, reflecting the family's distress.
  • 案定，人遂喧传 - When the case was settled, people began to spread the news, showing the public reaction.
  • 翁病之 - The old man was distressed by it, reflecting his emotional state.
  • 命子各出其妇 - He ordered his sons to send away their wives, a drastic measure to avoid further trouble.
  • 又皆不从 - But they all refused, showing the sons' resistance.
  • 乃徙于乡 - So he moved to a village, the old man's final decision.
  • 未及十余稔 - Before ten years had passed, setting a timeframe for the next event.
  • 而献忠之乱作 - The rebellion of Xianzong began, introducing a historical event.
  • 粉黛从军，为祸尤酷 - Women joined the army, and the disaster was especially severe, describing the impact of the rebellion.
  • 变鬼者或其兆之先见乎？ - Was the ghost a harbinger of the disaster? raising a question about the ghost's significance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - In Chinese culture, ghosts are often associated with supernatural beings that can cause harm or mischief.
  • 麻袋 - A burlap sack, commonly used for carrying goods or people in ancient times.
  • 珠还合浦 - A metaphor from the story of the pearl of Hainan, where a pearl was returned to its original place after being taken away. It symbolizes the return of something valuable.
  • 献忠之乱 - Refers to the rebellion led by Zhang Xianzhong, a Ming dynasty rebel leader who caused significant chaos and destruction in the late 17th century.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人而鬼乃其常也 - Literally means 'being a person and becoming a ghost is the norm,' suggesting that it is natural for people to become ghosts after death.
  • 鬼而实人则其变矣 - Literally means 'a ghost that actually behaves like a person is a change,' indicating that a ghost acting like a human is unusual.
  • 见之者固应动色 - Literally means 'those who see it should naturally be moved,' implying that seeing such a ghost should evoke a strong emotional response.
  • 至于搜囊箧辱闺人 - Literally means 'to search bags and boxes and humiliate women,' describing the aggressive and disrespectful behavior of the ghost.
  • 忍不起而与之争乎 - Literally means 'how can one not stand up and fight against it,' questioning the passivity of the victims.
  • 一老无论三子，皆壮夫 - Literally means 'one old man and his three sons, all strong men,' describing the family members who are supposed to be capable of defending themselves.
  • 何竟伈伈皔皔低首下心 - Literally means 'why do they still tremble and cower, bowing their heads and submitting,' criticizing their cowardice and submission.
  • 是可忍孰不可忍耶 - Literally means 'if this can be tolerated, what cannot be tolerated,' expressing the author's frustration and anger at their behavior.
  • 迨夫受侮不少 - Literally means 'when they have been insulted enough,' indicating a point of no return in the story.
  • 子且迁怒于仆 - Literally means 'the son will transfer his anger to the servant,' suggesting a shift in the dynamics of the family.
  • 父且命逐其妇 - Literally means 'the father will order the expulsion of his wife,' indicating extreme measures taken by the father.
  • 亦徒贻笑柄耳 - Literally means 'it will only become a laughing stock,' suggesting that their actions will be ridiculed.
  • 抑何补哉 - Literally means 'what benefit is there,' questioning the value of their actions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外史氏曰 - Common opening for commentary in classical texts, meaning 'The historian says.'
  • 鬼 - Ghosts in Chinese culture are often associated with the afterlife and can be benevolent or malevolent.
  • 闺人 - Refers to women, particularly those in the household, and is often used to denote a woman of virtue or modesty.
  • 仆 - Refers to a servant or low-ranking person.
  • 妇 - Refers to a wife or woman in a marital relationship.

Proper Nouns

  • 外史氏 - The historian or commentator, a generic term used in classical texts to introduce commentary.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 隱括其奸 - Literal meaning 'to conceal and bind its wickedness'; refers to the way the stories in *Liaozhai* expose and warn against deceitful practices.
  • 犀照怪 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like a rhinoceros horn revealing monsters'; indicates that the stories illuminate hidden truths or dangers.
  • 行旅所傳 - Refers to stories passed down among travelers, emphasizing the oral tradition of these tales.
  • 尤奇者 - Means 'the most extraordinary ones'; highlights the selection of particularly unusual or striking stories.
  • 禹鼎之一足 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'one leg of the great bronze tripod of Yu'; suggests that the selected stories serve as a small but significant part of a larger body of knowledge.
  • 俾客子知所趨避 - Means 'to make travelers know how to avoid danger'; emphasizes the practical advice contained in the stories.
  • 欲窺全豹猶未也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'one cannot yet see the whole leopard'; indicates that the selection only provides a glimpse of the broader subject.
  • 以功名爲重 - Means 'to prioritize official career and reputation'; reflects the societal values of the time.
  • 遣之赴都 - Means 'to send him to the capital'; indicates the importance of the capital in the social and political hierarchy.
  • 與其諸兄謀 - Means 'to consult with his elder brothers'; shows the family's involvement in the decision.
  • 將以求仕 - Means 'with the intention of seeking an official position'; indicates the purpose of the journey.
  • 濒行 - Means 'on the eve of departure'; sets the scene for the final instructions.
  • 予以千金 - Means 'gave him a thousand gold pieces'; indicates the financial support provided.
  • 僅資路費 - Means 'only to cover travel expenses'; suggests that the money was not meant for other purposes.
  • 以京中所需自足 - Means 'to be sufficient for what is needed in the capital'; indicates that the funds were intended to meet basic needs in the capital.
  • 猶慮其少未更事 - Means 'still worried that he was young and inexperienced'; shows the father's concern.
  • 嘱之曰 - Means 'told him'; introduces the father's final instructions.
  • 途次念秧者頗多 - Means 'there are many such scams on the road'; warns about the prevalence of fraud.
  • 汝此行不淫不賭不多事 - Means 'you will not engage in debauchery, gambling, or trouble'; sets expectations for the traveler's behavior.
  • 可以無患 - Means 'you can avoid trouble'; indicates the expected outcome of following the advice.
  • 切宜慎之 - Means 'you must be very careful'; emphasizes the importance of caution.
  • 公子謹受教 - Means 'the young man respectfully accepted the advice'; shows his compliance.
  • 紀綱數人 - Means 'a few attendants'; indicates the small entourage accompanying the traveler.
  • 載以巨艦 - Means 'traveling by a large ship'; suggests the scale of the journey.
  • 從以老僕 - Means 'accompanied by an old servant'; indicates the presence of a trusted companion.
  • 乘以匹馬 - Means 'riding a single horse'; suggests a more modest mode of travel.
  • 可以垂鉤而釣者也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'someone who can fish at will'; indicates the young man's independence and resourcefulness.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 方将细询 - Classical phrase meaning 'was about to inquire in detail.'
  • 适仆来请盥濯 - Literally 'just as the servant came to request washing,' indicating a sudden interruption.
  • 遂入室 - Means 'then entered the room,' marking a transition in the narrative.
  • 及昏 - Means 'by evening,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 田忽以盛馔入浼仆禀白 - Complex phrase meaning 'Tian suddenly brought in a sumptuous feast and asked the servant to report.'
  • 系公子长兄现莅某部斥革之吏 - Long phrase meaning 'it concerns the eldest brother of the young master, who is currently stationed at a certain department and has been dismissed from his post.'
  • 以故闲居在此 - Means 'therefore, he is staying here in leisure.'
  • 倘公子肯为缓颊 - Means 'if the young master would intercede on his behalf.'
  • 仍复厥役 - Means 'restore his position.'
  • 则不啻再造之恩矣 - Means 'it would be no less than a second life,' emphasizing the gratitude.
  • 因敬以盘飧上献 - Means 'therefore, respectfully offered a feast as a gift.'
  • 颇疑 - Means 'quite suspicious.'
  • 召入问之 - Means 'called him in to question him.'
  • 凿凿叙述 - Means 'stated clearly and convincingly.'
  • 其事甚细 - Means 'the matter is quite detailed.'
  • 其情可原 - Means 'his situation is deserving of leniency.'
  • 诘其何以出京 - Means 'asked why he had left the capital.'
  • 又对曰 - Means 'he replied again.'
  • 吏之兄实亦小吏 - Means 'the brother of the official is also a minor official.'
  • 现掌案卷于县 - Means 'currently manages records in the county.'
  • 故来依之 - Means 'therefore, he came to rely on him.'
  • 公子又私以询仆 - Means 'the young master then privately inquired of the servant.'
  • 则逆旅内外，人多识之 - Means 'within and outside the inn, many people recognize him.'
  • 因亦坦然不疑 - Means 'therefore, he became completely at ease and no longer doubted.'
  • 三辞而后受之 - Means 'after three refusals, he finally accepted it.'
  • 更加慰劳 - Means 'further comforted and rewarded him.'
  • 许为排解 - Means 'promised to mediate.'
  • 田色似甚喜 - Means 'Tian's expression seemed very pleased.'
  • 顿首至地 - Means 'knelt down with his head touching the ground,' a gesture of deep gratitude.
  • 感激不胜 - Means 'overwhelmed with gratitude.'
  • 公子遂独酌于旅舍 - Means 'the young master then drank alone in the inn.'
  • 田与群仆呼卢浮白 - Means 'Tian and the group of servants played dice and drank freely.'
  • 亦乐而未央 - Means 'also enjoying themselves without end.'
  • 酒将半 - Means 'when the wine was half consumed.'
  • 又有客至 - Means 'another guest arrived.'
  • 人骑喧嘶 - Means 'people and horses made loud noises.'
  • 遽问公子宿处 - Means 'quickly asked about the young master's lodging.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公子 - Refers to a young noble or gentleman.
  • 斥革之吏 - Refers to an official who has been dismissed from his post, a common occurrence in imperial China.
  • 逆旅 - Refers to an inn or lodging house.
  • 呼卢浮白 - Refers to a traditional Chinese drinking game, where players take turns drinking and betting.
  • 顿首 - A traditional bowing gesture, often used in formal or respectful contexts.
  • 酒将半 - Indicates a specific moment in a drinking session, often associated with social gatherings.

Proper Nouns

  • 公子 - The young master or gentleman.
  • 田 - The name of the servant or attendant.
  • 某部 - A specific department or office, though the exact location is not specified.
  • 县 - A county, though the specific location is not specified.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 念秧者至矣 - Metaphorical expression referring to a type of swindler or cheat known as 'niànyāng' (念秧). Often associated with gambling and other deceptive practices.
  • 桑梓 - Literary device referring to one's hometown or ancestral place, evoking a sense of home and origin.
  • 叨陪待 - Polite expression meaning 'to humbly attend' or 'to be honored to accompany,' expressing the speaker's presence in a social setting.
  • 衣带水 - Metaphor for a very short distance, often used to describe a small body of water separating two places.
  • 未获识荆 - Polite expression meaning 'have not had the privilege of meeting,' derived from the story of '识荆' (zhì jīng), where a person meets someone of high status.
  • 北上 - Refers to traveling north, often implying a journey to a more prestigious or central location.
  • 三生之幸 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'a great fortune in three lifetimes,' expressing a sense of extreme good luck or happiness.
  • 叨登乡榜 - Refers to passing the local examination and being listed on the honor roll.
  • 丁艰 - Refers to a period of mourning, typically for a parent, during which one could not participate in official duties or examinations.
  • 部选 - Refers to the selection process by the Ministry of Personnel for official appointments, a crucial part of the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 守候至今 - Means 'waiting until now,' indicating a prolonged period of waiting.
  • 保府试用 - Refers to being selected for a temporary position in a provincial government, often a stepping stone to higher offices.
  • 宦途可谓蹭蹬 - Means 'one's career has been quite difficult or slow,' referring to a lack of progress in one's official career.
  • 藉此遇兄 - Means 'thanks to this, I have met my elder brother,' expressing gratitude for the current encounter.
  • 大快人意 - Means 'very pleasing' or 'very satisfying,' indicating a strong positive feeling.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某对公子笑曰 - Placeholder for a person's name used humbly, followed by 'the person smiled at the young master and said.'
  • 弟自北来，适遭兄之前驱 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'happened to meet the young master's advance,' suggesting the speaker encountered the young master before he arrived.
  • 知今夕当宿此地 - Indicates that the speaker knew the young master would be staying at the place that night.
  • 故跟寻而至 - Suggests that the speaker followed the young master and arrived at the place.

  • 公子不禁动念 - Indicates that the young master could not help but think of something.

  • 然不以亵尊为嫌，弟一人正苦寂寞，何不共居此室，抵足谈心耶 - Suggests that the young master does not consider it disrespectful to share the room and talks heart-to-heart.
  • 某大喜，谢曰 - Indicates that the speaker was very happy and thanked the young master.
  • 暮夜良难转移 - Suggests that it is indeed difficult to move during the late night.
  • 但弟武人鲁莽，或不便于兄，反取罪戾 - Suggests that the young master is concerned that the speaker, being a rough military man, might be inconvenient and incur blame.
  • 既蒙盛谊挽留，弟亦不敢自外，谨受命 - Indicates that the young master has received great favor and the speaker respectfully accepts the command.
  • 即呼从人将衣装尽卸室内，竟不复迁 - Describes the speaker's action of unloading all the clothes inside the room and not moving again.
  • 公子覸其豪华，迥殊寒素，与己亦不甚相悬，愈无所猜忌，乃洗盏更酌 - Indicates that the young master saw the speaker's luxury, which was very different from the poor, but not much different from himself, and even more without suspicion, then washed the cups and poured more wine.
  • 又有顷，田入行酒，公子之侍从踵至 - Describes the situation where the wine steward served the wine and the young master's attendants followed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公子 - Refers to a young nobleman or a young master, often used in Classical Chinese to denote a young man of high social status.
  • 武人 - Refers to a military man or a warrior, often used to describe someone who is strong and rough.
  • 鲁莽 - Describes someone who is rash or impulsive, often used to describe a military man who is not refined.
  • 寒素 - Refers to someone who is poor and simple, often used to contrast with the luxury of the rich.
  • 洗盏更酌 - A ritual of washing the cups and pouring more wine, often used in Classical Chinese to describe a refined and graceful act.
  • 田入行酒 - Refers to the wine steward serving the wine, a common practice in ancient Chinese banquets.
  • 公子之侍从踵至 - Refers to the young master's attendants following, indicating the arrival of other guests or servants.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '某对公子笑曰：‘弟自北来，适遭兄之前驱，知今夕当宿此地，故跟寻而至，冀得一席之地，可以少领清谈。今竟不能，殊觉怅怅。’' can be broken down into several parts to make it clearer.

  • The sequence '妇人排闼入室，命小鬟以烛照之，微哂曰' describes the woman entering the room and giving instructions to the maid, followed by her speaking with a slight smile.

  • The sequence '言讫，北面就坐，呼童以礼相见' indicates that the woman finishes speaking, sits down facing north, and calls the child to greet him formally.
  • The phrase '潜睨其容，黛绿粉白，犹存风韵' describes the man discreetly looking at the woman's appearance, noting her makeup and her remaining grace.
  • The thought '窃计，巨鬼畏此，此必其中巨擘' suggests that the man thinks the powerful ghost fears this woman, implying she is a leading figure.
  • The explanation '予受此剑，本未一试，顷因气馁，致为鬼所揶揄' means the man received the sword but had not yet tested it, and his lack of confidence led to being mocked by the ghost.
  • The observation '见妇人指挥从婢，似将不利于己，意殊莫测' indicates that the man observes the woman commanding her maids, suspecting that she might be planning something harmful, and finds her intentions mysterious.
  • The action '乃俯掇其剑，挺而刺之' describes the man picking up the sword and stabbing her.
  • The change in demeanor '妇人忽回眸一笑曰' indicates the woman suddenly turns her head and smiles, suggesting a shift in her attitude.
  • The statement '予以好生，不戕汝命，且欲讲济世之术' means the woman values life and does not want to kill him, and instead wants to teach him a method to save the world.
  • The accusation '汝乃包藏祸心，反以予试此刃，是诚不可救药者矣' accuses the man of having hidden malicious intentions and testing her with the sword, concluding that he is beyond help.
  • The challenge '汝既有利器，请断予首。倘不能效，弗可活也' challenges the man to cut her head off with the sword, threatening that if he fails, he will not survive.

  • 砚席之同者，莫不闻而讶之 - Phrase describing the reaction of those studying with him, literally 'Those who studied with him could not help but hear and be astonished.'
  • 诘以其故，坚不肯言 - Phrase describing Zhu's refusal to explain, literally 'When questioned about the reason, he firmly refused to speak.'
  • 阅数日，院师开讲，众皆肃听，朱独漠然不闻 - Phrase describing the contrast between Zhu and others during a lecture, literally 'After several days, the academy teacher began to lecture, and everyone listened respectfully, but Zhu alone was indifferent and did not hear.'
  • 时时如与人偶语 - Phrase describing Zhu's behavior as if talking to someone, literally 'He often behaved as if talking to someone.'
  • 师怪而诘之，乃缄口木立，少间复然 - Phrase describing the teacher's reaction and Zhu's response, literally 'The teacher was surprised and questioned him, but he remained silent and stood like a wooden statue, and soon after, he acted the same way again.'
  • 有与之同日游者，因陈其事于师 - Phrase describing someone who shared the same day of study informing the teacher, literally 'Someone who studied with him on the same day reported the matter to the teacher.'
  • 因此辍讲，命人伴送使归，备述其详 - Phrase describing the teacher's decision and actions, literally 'Therefore, the lecture was stopped, and someone was ordered to accompany him home, and they reported the details in full.'
  • 父母皆垂泣，朱独摇首止之 - Phrase describing the parents' reaction and Zhu's response, literally 'Both parents shed tears, but Zhu alone shook his head to stop them.'

  • 前仿御沟流水 - 前 means 'before,' 仿 means 'imitate,' 御沟 means 'imperial canal,' and 流水 means 'flowing water.' Together, it means 'before imitating the flowing water of the imperial canal.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '乃行未二日程，有妇人靓妆俟于路，逆而言曰' introduces the setting and the woman's appearance and speech in a compact form.
  • The woman's speech '久侍巾栉，何忍遐弃，胡不签妻同配？将焉置妾耶？' is a series of rhetorical questions expressing her feelings and demands.
  • The clause '役方疑其有室，而仆早知其为狐' contrasts the official's doubt with the servant's knowledge.
  • The servant's speech '累若扶持，既居缧絏，又荷成戈，今更依依弗舍' outlines his current situation and reluctance to leave.
  • The servant offers a conditional solution to the woman's demand: '然欲偕行赴配，予实无计资生。若能为钱树子，则可共赡。否即请回玉趾'.
  • The fox's reaction is described in a vivid and dramatic manner: '狐闻言红涨于面，唾曰：“是儿尖嘴，竟至死不改。”直前以手扪其吻，血出如濡'.
  • The aftermath of the fox's attack is described in detail: '视狐已不见，二役惊顾仓皇。亟视仆，则唇之上下割裂半寸许，如十字然'.
  • The servant and the officials react to the event and understand its cause: '骇而且笑，相偕东行。归为公家人述之，始悟前此之事，皆狐之报其戏也'.
  • The servant's future and the fate of the fox are described: '后数年，仆以大赦还，得仍为公家服役，但绝口不敢言狐。而公家之狐，早不知其安往也'.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '随园老人曰：遇此人，不得不销此魂' is a direct quote from the 'Old Man of Sui Garden,' emphasizing the impact of meeting a certain person.
  • The statement '浩歌子之言，真是情至之语' praises the emotional depth of Haogeshizi's words.
  • The clause '情之所钟，犹在我辈' suggests that deep emotions are still felt by the speaker and his peers.
  • The phrase '素健讼' indicates that the person is skilled in legal matters.
  • The sequence '刀笔之能，历干守令，咸莫能屈' describes the person's skill in legal writing and his frequent dealings with officials, none of whom could defeat him.
  • The sentence '关中大疫，死者莫数，其父叔亦以疫卒' describes a major epidemic in Guanzhong, resulting in many deaths, including the person's father and uncle.
  • The sequence '大恚，投词于城隍，备诋疫鬼之暴' describes the person's anger and his submission of a petition to the City God, criticizing the cruelty of the epidemic spirits.
  • The phrase '凡数百言，词语激切' indicates that the petition was written in several hundred words and was forceful and urgent.
  • The sentence '一日，梦城隍召之去，当庭责问，色似甚怒' describes a dream in which the person was summoned by the City God, who questioned him angrily.
  • The clause '天灾流行，实亦人所自致，汝何喋喋如此' argues that natural disasters are also caused by human actions and questions why the person keeps talking like this.
  • The sequence '明神，权操于上帝，予且不能左右于其间' describes the power of the bright and wise spirits and the Heavenly God, and the person's inability to control the situation.
  • The clause '草莽小民，竟敢以狂言相怼耶' criticizes the common people for daring to defy with reckless words.
  • The sequence '然！言者诚为有罪，但人生寿夭有命，岂于疫而独无命耶' acknowledges the fault of speaking but argues that life and death have their destiny, and the epidemic cannot be an exception.
  • The conditional statement '若有命在，何死者命皆当夭，夭者偏皆遇疫' questions why the dead all die prematurely and those who die prematurely all encounter the epidemic.
  • The conditional statement '如云无命，又何以有造生造死之说' questions how there can be talk of creating life and death if there is no destiny.
  • The question '岂先造疫，而后造命乎' asks if the epidemic is created first and then the destiny.
  • The question '抑不必造命，而独造疫乎' asks if destiny is not created, but the epidemic is.
  • The sequence '是不可解，因以兴讼，惟神有以教之' concludes that the situation is incomprehensible, so the person brings a lawsuit, hoping that the gods can teach him.

  • 揖刘而谢曰 - 揖 is a formal bow or gesture of respect, and 谢 means to thank or to express gratitude.
  • 赖君片言 - 赖 means thanks to or because of, and 片言 means a few words or a brief statement.
  • 吾曹皆免鬼录 - 吾曹 means we or us, and 鬼录 refers to the Book of the Dead, implying that they have been spared from death.
  • 将何以酬 - 将何以 is a rhetorical question meaning how can we repay, and 酬 means to repay or to reward.
  • 俱一二日中物化者 - 物化 means to transform into something else, often used to describe death or passing away.
  • 刘亦以失路告之 - 失路 means to lose one's way or to be lost.
  • 众乃导以同归 - 导 means to guide, and 同归 means to return together.
  • 甫行半里许 - 甫 means just or only, and 半里许 means half a li (a unit of distance).
  • 突遭厉鬼三四辈 - 突遭 means suddenly encountered, and 厉鬼 refers to fierce or malevolent ghosts.
  • 虬筋鹘目 - 虬筋 describes twisted or knotted muscles, and 鹘目 means hawk-like eyes, suggesting fierce or predatory features.
  • 吼声如恶盋 - 恶盋 means a loud, harsh sound, often used to describe a scream or roar.
  • 见刘皆甚怒 - 见 means upon seeing, and 甚怒 means very angry.
  • 相属争以巨爪攫拿 - 相属 means to follow one another, and 攫拿 means to seize or grab with claws.
  • 众遂如鸟兽散 - 如鸟兽散 means to scatter like birds and beasts, describing a chaotic dispersal.
  • 刘独坦然揖之 - 独坦然 means alone and calmly, and 揖之 means bowing to them.
  • 公等即疫鬼耶 - 公等 means you all or sirs, and 疫鬼 refers to epidemic spirits or ghosts causing disease.
  • 吾父死于是，吾叔死于是 - 于是 means because of this or due to this, referring to the cause of death.
  • 所以忘死而与公等讼者 - 所以 means the reason why, 忘死 means to forget death, and 讼 means to argue or to contend.
  • 诚以好生者天，正直者神 - 诚以 means because, 好生者天 means the heavens favor life, and 正直者神 means the gods uphold righteousness.
  • 公等奉行不善，罪且莫逭 - 奉行不善 means to carry out poorly, and 罪且莫逭 means the sin cannot be avoided.
  • 予纵死亦不为公等屈 - 纵 means even if, and 屈 means to yield or to submit.
  • 鬼闻之，相顾愕然 - 相顾 means to look at each other, and 愕然 means startled or astonished.
  • 其威顿霁 - 顿霁 means suddenly calmed or suddenly softened.
  • 公等奔波亦良苦 - 奔波 means to travel back and forth, and 良苦 means very hard or very difficult.
  • 莫能血食 - 莫能 means cannot, and 血食 refers to the blood sacrifice or the sustenance of the spirits.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 疫鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts associated with epidemics, a common belief in Chinese folklore.
  • 鬼录 - Refers to the Book of the Dead, a concept from Chinese mythology where the dead are recorded.
  • 物化 - A philosophical term from Daoism, meaning transformation into something else, often used to describe death.
  • 片言 - A term from classical rhetoric, meaning a few words or a brief statement, often used to emphasize the power of concise speech.
  • 同里 - Refers to people from the same village or neighborhood, emphasizing community ties.
  • 联袖 - A poetic term describing people walking together, often with their sleeves linked, symbolizing unity or companionship.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 方贸然前行，俄有同里数人，联袖而来，面容大有喜色。This sequence describes the protagonist's movement, followed by the sudden appearance of people from the same village, walking together with a joyful expression.
  • 揖刘而谢曰：“赖君片言，吾曹皆免鬼录，将何以酬？”This clause introduces a formal bow and a thank-you, followed by a question about how to repay the protagonist.
  • 盖俱一二日中物化者。This clause explains that the people are those who died within the past one or two days.
  • 刘亦以失路告之，众乃导以同归。This clause describes the protagonist explaining that he is lost, and the group guides him back.
  • 甫行半里许，突遭厉鬼三四辈，虬筋鹘目，吼声如恶盋。This clause describes the sudden encounter with fierce ghosts, emphasizing their physical appearance and sound.
  • 见刘皆甚怒，相属争以巨爪攫拿。This clause describes the ghosts' reaction to the protagonist, showing their anger and aggressive behavior.
  • 众遂如鸟兽散，刘独坦然揖之曰：“公等即疫鬼耶？吾父死于是，吾叔死于是，与某而将三矣。所以忘死而与公等讼者，诚以好生者天，正直者神，公等奉行不善，罪且莫逭。予纵死亦不为公等屈。”This long sequence describes the group's dispersal, the protagonist's calm response, and his argument with the ghosts.
  • 鬼闻之，相顾愕然，其威顿霁。This clause describes the ghosts' reaction to the protagonist's words, showing their sudden calm.
  • 刘因曰：“公等奔波亦良苦，且时致人怨，莫能血食。”This clause describes the protagonist's further words to the ghosts, expressing sympathy and understanding.

  • 乃跽而言曰 - Literally 'then knelt and said'; describes the concubine's act of kneeling and speaking.
  • 贼之所悦者，色也 - Literally 'what the bandits like is beauty'; indicates the bandits' preference for beauty.
  • 儿有毁容之术 - Literally 'I have a method to disfigure myself'; refers to the concubine's plan to disfigure herself.
  • 使贼不可以近身 - Literally 'make the bandits unable to approach'; suggests the concubine's intention to deter the bandits.
  • 不愈于舍亲而就死乎 - Literally 'is it not better to abandon one's relatives and die?'; a rhetorical question about the concubine's sacrifice.
  • 父母亦不忍予 - Literally 'parents also could not bear to let me die'; indicates the parents' reluctance to sacrifice the concubine.
  • 遂听之 - Literally 'thus they agreed'; describes the parents' agreement to the concubine's plan.
  • 预储人犬之矢及秽污等物 - Literally 'previously stored human and dog feces and other filth'; refers to the concubine's preparation of materials for disfigurement.
  • 闻城将陷 - Literally 'heard that the city was about to fall'; indicates the concubine's awareness of the impending danger.
  • 先以炭漆其身 - Literally 'first applied charcoal and lacquer to her body'; describes the concubine's initial step in disfiguring herself.
  • 继以垢泥重以溲便 - Literally 'then covered with dirt and urine'; continues the description of the concubine's disfigurement.
  • 径扶父母出奔 - Literally 'directly supported the parents in fleeing'; describes the concubine's action in helping her parents escape.
  • 虽乱军之际 - Literally 'even amidst the chaos of the army'; sets the scene of the escape during a chaotic military situation.
  • 白刃交挥 - Literally 'white blades were slashing'; describes the violent and chaotic environment.
  • 见妾以为狂 - Literally 'seeing me, they thought I was mad'; indicates the bandits' reaction to the concubine's appearance.
  • 曾不一盼 - Literally 'not even once glanced'; emphasizes the bandits' disregard for the concubine.
  • 嗣是父母失其产 - Literally 'afterward, the parents lost their property'; describes the consequences of the escape.
  • 又染痼疾 - Literally 'also contracted a chronic illness'; indicates the parents' subsequent health issues.
  • 尝在床蓐 - Literally 'often lay on the bed'; describes the parents' state of illness.
  • 以此亲往贼营求乞以养 - Literally 'therefore personally went to the bandits' camp to beg for food to support them'; describes the concubine's efforts to provide for her parents.
  • 贼恒怜妾 - Literally 'the bandits always pitied me'; indicates the bandits' ongoing sympathy for the concubine.
  • 而从无邪心 - Literally 'and never had any evil intentions'; emphasizes the concubine's purity.
  • 呼妾曰“癫人” - Literally 'called me a 'crazy person''; describes the bandits' derogatory nickname for the concubine.
  • 时以口食与之 - Literally 'often gave me food'; indicates the bandits' provision of food to the concubine.
  • 如是者半岁 - Literally 'like this for half a year'; describes the duration of the concubine's situation.
  • 贼退而父亡 - Literally 'after the bandits retreated, the father died'; indicates the father's death following the bandits' retreat.
  • 妾负母丐于四方 - Literally 'I carried the mother and begged everywhere'; describes the concubine's efforts to support her mother.
  • 深惧宵小 - Literally 'deeply feared thieves'; indicates the concubine's fear of small-time criminals.
  • 因以不更 - Literally 'thus did not change'; suggests the concubine's decision to maintain her appearance.
  • 今春，母又见背 - Literally 'this spring, the mother passed away again'; indicates the mother's death.
  • 孑然只影 - Literally 'alone with one shadow'; describes the concubine's solitary state.
  • 愈不敢稍露其形 - Literally 'even more dared not slightly reveal her appearance'; emphasizes the concubine's caution.

  • 此亦周官议贵之典也 - This is also in line with the Zhou official's regulations for nobles; '周官' (zhōu guān) refers to the officials of the Zhou dynasty, and '议贵' (yì guì) means 'regulations for nobles'.

  • 未及期，果得其奸状 - Before the deadline, he indeed discovered the criminal behavior. This shows the success of the investigation.
  • 盖邑有豪家，素习强横 - It turned out that there was a powerful family in the county who had always been arrogant and domineering. This provides context for the criminal behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 故明之循吏也 - Refers to officials who followed the law and governed with integrity during the Ming dynasty.
  • 举司衙务 - Describes the role of the two brothers in handling administrative duties.
  • 人皆以大小钟别之 - A colloquial way of distinguishing between the two brothers.
  • 方公 - Refers to Mr. Fang, the official.
  • 某县令 - Refers to the position of county magistrate.
  • 历任丞牧 - Refers to the positions of prefect or governor.
  • 僚友 - Refers to colleagues and friends.
  • 疑狱 - Refers to a doubtful or unresolved case.
  • 揭宰 - Refers to reporting to the governor.
  • 土人 - Refers to the locals.
  • 攻苦 - Refers to enduring hardship.
  • 克出宰百里 - Refers to becoming a magistrate of a hundred li.
  • 某令 - Refers to the magistrate.
  • 仓卒难明 - Refers to the difficulty of clarifying the matter quickly.
  • 豪家 - Refers to a powerful family.
  • 素习强横 - Refers to the family's habitual arrogance and domineering behavior.
